questions.question_text: Which of the following is the BEST definition of real property?
questions.correct_answer: Land and everything permanently attached to it
distractors: Any tangible item that can be moved between locations
Buildings and structures only, excluding the land
Property that has been sold and publicly recorded
questions.explanation: Real property consists of the land and everything permanently attached to it, including buildings, improvements, and fixtures. Items that are movable are personal property (also called chattels). The distinction matters in real estate because only real property is transferred by deed.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller are negotiating a home sale. The seller wants to take the freestanding kitchen refrigerator. The buyer argues it should stay. Who is MOST likely correct?
questions.correct_answer: The seller, because a freestanding refrigerator is personal property
distractors: The buyer, because all kitchen appliances are real property
The seller, but only if the refrigerator was recently purchased
The buyer, because everything inside the home conveys with the deed
questions.explanation: A freestanding refrigerator is not permanently attached to the structure — it is plugged into an outlet and can be moved without damage. It is personal property belonging to the seller. Built-in appliances (dishwashers, wall ovens, built-in microwaves) are different because they are permanently attached and become fixtures. The sales contract should always specify which personal property items, if any, are included.

questions.question_text: Which mnemonic is commonly used to remember the tests for determining whether an item is a fixture?
questions.correct_answer: MARIA
distractors: DUST
PITI
OLDCAR
questions.explanation: MARIA stands for: Method of attachment, Adaptability of the item to the property's use, Relationship of the parties (buyer/seller vs. landlord/tenant), Intent of the party who attached it, and Agreement between the parties. Of these, intent is often the most decisive factor. DUST (Demand, Utility, Scarcity, Transferability) describes value; PITI describes mortgage payments; OLDCAR describes fiduciary duties.

questions.question_text: A seller installed a custom built-in bookcase spanning an entire living room wall, bolted into the wall studs. The listing agent should treat this bookcase as:
questions.correct_answer: A fixture included in the sale unless excluded in the contract
distractors: Personal property the seller may remove before closing
A fixture only if the buyer requests it in writing
Personal property because shelving is never real property
questions.explanation: A built-in bookcase bolted to wall studs meets multiple MARIA criteria: it is permanently attached (method of attachment) and adapted to the property's use. Courts and buyers typically treat such items as fixtures included with the real estate. To remove it, the seller must explicitly exclude it in the listing agreement and purchase contract. Failure to disclose its removal can constitute a material fact violation.

questions.question_text: A residential tenant installs a ceiling fan in their apartment. The lease is silent about fixtures. When the lease ends, who owns the ceiling fan?
questions.correct_answer: The landlord, because the fan became a fixture
distractors: The tenant, because they purchased and installed it
Neither party owns it separately
The tenant, because tenants retain items they install
questions.explanation: A ceiling fan permanently wired into the electrical system and attached to the ceiling is a fixture — it has become part of the real property. Without a written agreement to the contrary, residential fixtures installed by a tenant become the landlord's property. This differs from trade fixtures (commercial use), which tenants may remove. Tenants should always get written permission and a fixture agreement before making improvements.

questions.question_text: How do trade fixtures differ from ordinary fixtures in real property law?
questions.correct_answer: They remain the tenant's personal property, removable at lease end
distractors: They are permanently attached and become part of the real estate
They can only be removed with the landlord's written permission
They are items installed by the landlord for the tenant's business
questions.explanation: Trade fixtures are items of personal property installed by a commercial tenant to conduct their business (shelving, equipment, signs, display cases). Unlike residential fixtures, trade fixtures are an exception to the general rule — they remain the tenant's personal property. The tenant has the right to remove them before or at lease expiration but must repair any resulting damage. If left behind after lease expiration, they may become the landlord's property through abandonment.

questions.question_text: A dry-cleaning tenant bolts industrial cleaning machines to the floor and installs ventilation ducts through the wall. The lease expires. The tenant's RIGHT regarding this equipment is:
questions.correct_answer: Remove the equipment as trade fixtures, repairing any damage
distractors: Leave the equipment because it is permanently attached
Remove it only with the landlord's written consent at removal
Forfeit the equipment, which became the landlord's property
questions.explanation: Industrial cleaning equipment, even when bolted to the floor or connected to ducts, qualifies as a trade fixture because it is part of the tenant's business operations. The tenant retains ownership and has the right to remove it. However, the tenant must repair all damage caused by the removal (e.g., fill bolt holes, patch walls). If the tenant leaves the equipment after lease expiration without agreement, it may be treated as abandoned and become the landlord's property.

questions.question_text: Emblements (fructus industriales) are BEST described as:
questions.correct_answer: Annual crops cultivated by a tenant, classified as personal property
distractors: Naturally occurring trees and perennial plants growing on the land
Timber rights sold separately from the underlying fee estate
Crops that become real estate once harvested and stored on site
questions.explanation: Emblements (from the Latin fructus industriales, 'fruits of industry') are annual crops planted and tended by a tenant. Because they result from the tenant's labor and expense, they are classified as personal property — not part of the real estate. The tenant retains ownership and the right to harvest them. Naturally occurring vegetation like trees and perennials (fructus naturales) is real property.

questions.question_text: A farmer leases farmland and plants wheat. Midway through the growing season, the landlord dies and the estate is sold to a new owner. What happens to the wheat crop?
questions.correct_answer: The farmer retains the right to re-enter and harvest
distractors: The wheat transfers to the new owner with the land
The farmer loses the crop when the landlord dies
The farmer must negotiate a new lease to harvest
questions.explanation: The doctrine of emblements protects tenant farmers whose tenancy ends through no fault of their own. When a tenancy terminates due to circumstances beyond the tenant's control (such as the landlord's death or sale), the tenant retains the right to re-enter and harvest crops they planted. The wheat, as emblements (fructus industriales), is the tenant's personal property and does not transfer to the new owner with the real estate. This doctrine prevents unjust enrichment of new landowners.

questions.question_text: A homeowner cuts down a mature oak tree in their backyard and has it milled into lumber. The lumber is now classified as:
questions.correct_answer: Personal property, because severance converted it from real property
distractors: Real property, because the lumber originated from that parcel
Personal property only if the owner paid a severance tax
Real property until sold or transported off the premises
questions.explanation: Severance is the process by which real property becomes personal property. While the tree was standing and rooted in the ground, it was part of the real estate. Once cut down (severed from the land), it became personal property — the lumber. The reverse process is annexation, which converts personal property into real property (e.g., buying bricks at a store, then permanently mortaring them into a retaining wall).

questions.question_text: A buyer contracts to purchase a home. Before closing, the seller removes three large, established arborvitae trees from the yard, claiming they were personal property because the seller originally planted them as small nursery stock. The buyer's STRONGEST argument that the trees should have remained is:
questions.correct_answer: Once planted, the trees became real property through annexation
distractors: State law prohibits removing trees before closing
The seller should have documented the trees as personal property
Trees have no market value, giving the buyer no valid claim
questions.explanation: Annexation converts personal property into real property. When the seller originally purchased young nursery trees (personal property) and permanently planted them in the ground, they became part of the real estate through annexation. As established trees rooted in the soil, they were real property. The seller had no unilateral right to sever and remove them after entering into a purchase contract. The buyer can claim breach of contract and/or seek damages for the diminution in value caused by their removal. The sales contract should have explicitly excluded them for the seller to have a right to remove them.

questions.question_text: A residential sale includes the home and the seller's riding lawnmower. The deed transfers the home. How should the lawnmower transfer be documented?
questions.correct_answer: By a separate bill of sale at closing
distractors: By listing the lawnmower on the deed as an attachment
No documentation is needed
By adding the lawnmower to the deed as a chattel addendum
questions.explanation: A deed is the legal instrument used exclusively to transfer real property. Personal property is transferred by a separate bill of sale. Including personal property on a deed is improper and could create title issues. The purchase contract should identify all included personal property items, and a bill of sale formally transfers them at closing. This distinction also matters for tax and title insurance purposes.

questions.question_text: A homeowner's wall-to-wall carpeting is stapled to the subfloor and cut to fit each room precisely. A buyer asks if it conveys with the sale. The carpeting is MOST likely classified as:
questions.correct_answer: Real property, because it is attached and adapted to the space
distractors: Personal property, because floor coverings are movable furnishings
Real property only if disclosed on a fixture addendum
Personal property, because it was purchased from a retail store
questions.explanation: Wall-to-wall carpeting stapled to the subfloor and custom-cut to fit the rooms is permanently attached and adapted to the property. It meets fixture tests and is generally treated as real property that conveys with the sale. Area rugs, by contrast, sit loose on the floor and remain personal property. The key distinction is the method and permanence of attachment.

questions.question_text: An owner sells undeveloped land. Which of the following would NOT transfer with the deed as part of the real property?
questions.correct_answer: A portable storage shed sitting on concrete blocks
distractors: Underground mineral deposits beneath the surface
A gravel driveway graded and compacted into the soil
Mature trees growing naturally along the property line
questions.explanation: Real property includes the land, subsurface minerals, naturally growing vegetation, and permanent improvements. A portable storage shed resting on blocks without a foundation is not permanently attached — it can be lifted and moved. This makes it personal property. Underground minerals are part of the land itself, a graded driveway is an improvement to the land, and natural trees are fructus naturales (real property).

questions.question_text: Which term describes items of personal property that are movable and not attached to real estate?
questions.correct_answer: Chattels
distractors: Emblements
Appurtenances
Encumbrances
questions.explanation: Chattels is the legal term for movable personal property — items that are not permanently attached to real estate. Emblements are annual crops (a specific type of personal property). Appurtenances are rights that run with the land (like easements). Encumbrances are claims or liens against real property. Knowing these distinctions is essential for classifying items in a real estate transaction.

questions.question_text: In determining whether an item is a fixture, courts generally consider the intent of the party who attached it to be the MOST important factor. Which of the MARIA tests reflects this principle?
questions.correct_answer: Intent of the annexor
distractors: Method of attachment
Agreement of the parties
Relationship of the parties
questions.explanation: While all five MARIA factors are considered, courts consistently hold that the intent of the party who attached the item is the single most important factor. Intent is judged objectively — not by what the person says they intended, but by what a reasonable person would conclude from the circumstances. Method of attachment provides evidence of intent, but intent itself is the controlling factor.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller dispute whether a window air conditioning unit is a fixture. The unit sits in the window frame held by gravity and an adjustable bracket, with no screws or permanent modification to the window. Applying the MARIA test, this unit is MOST likely:
questions.correct_answer: Personal property, not permanently attached
distractors: A fixture, adapted to cool that specific room
A fixture, because it uses the property's electrical outlet
Personal property only if purchased within the current year
questions.explanation: Under MARIA, the method of attachment is a key indicator. A window AC unit held by gravity and a bracket — with no screws, bolts, or permanent modification — lacks permanent attachment. It can be removed without any damage to the property. While it is adapted to cooling the room, the lack of permanent attachment and the ease of removal indicate the intent was not to make it a permanent part of the real estate. Central HVAC systems, by contrast, are fixtures because they are permanently installed.

questions.question_text: A restaurant tenant installs a commercial walk-in cooler that is wired into the building's electrical panel and bolted to the concrete slab. The tenant's lease expires and the tenant wants to take the cooler. If the lease is silent on fixtures, the tenant's BEST argument is:
questions.correct_answer: A trade fixture removable by the tenant with damage repaired
distractors: Personal property because purchased with the tenant's funds
Real property because it is bolted to the slab permanently
Property of whichever party files a fixture claim first
questions.explanation: Even though the walk-in cooler is heavily attached (bolted to slab, wired into electrical), trade fixture law favors commercial tenants. Items installed by a business tenant to operate their business are trade fixtures — an exception to the general rule that attached items become real property. The tenant may remove them before or at lease expiration but must repair damage. This rule encourages commercial tenants to invest in their business spaces without fear of losing equipment.

questions.question_text: A commercial tenant's lease expires on March 31. The tenant vacates on April 15 without removing custom display shelving installed for their retail business. The landlord wants to keep the shelving. The landlord's STRONGEST position is:
questions.correct_answer: The tenant abandoned the fixtures by failing to remove them
distractors: Trade fixtures automatically become the landlord's at lease expiration
The landlord must store them 90 days before claiming ownership
The tenant retains ownership and can retrieve them at any time
questions.explanation: A tenant's right to remove trade fixtures is not unlimited — it must be exercised before or at the time the lease expires. When a tenant leaves trade fixtures behind after lease expiration without making arrangements, the fixtures are considered abandoned and become the landlord's property. This is why commercial tenants should coordinate fixture removal well before the lease end date. Some leases include specific removal deadlines or require the tenant to restore the premises.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the BEST example of a trade fixture?
questions.correct_answer: A dental chair bolted to the floor of a leased office
distractors: A ceiling fan installed by a residential tenant
A built-in closet system added by a homeowner before listing
A furnace that is part of the building's central heating system
questions.explanation: A dental chair installed by a commercial tenant for their dental practice is a classic trade fixture — business equipment attached to the premises for conducting the tenant's trade. A ceiling fan installed by a residential tenant is an ordinary fixture (not a trade fixture, because it's residential). A built-in closet system is a fixture attached by the owner. A furnace is part of the building's mechanical system and belongs to the property owner.

questions.question_text: A tenant farmer plants soybeans in April on leased land. In August, the landowner sells the property. The new owner demands the farmer leave immediately. The farmer's soybean crop is classified as:
questions.correct_answer: Personal property of the farmer, who may harvest it
distractors: Real property that transferred to the new owner by deed
Personal property only with a written crop agreement
Real property until harvest, then the new owner's personal property
questions.explanation: Soybeans are annual crops planted through the farmer's labor, making them emblements (fructus industriales) — classified as personal property of the tenant. The doctrine of emblements gives the tenant the right to return and harvest crops when the tenancy ends involuntarily. The new landowner received the land but not the tenant's crops. This protects farmers from losing their investment when leases end unexpectedly.

questions.question_text: What is the key distinction between fructus industriales and fructus naturales?
questions.correct_answer: Industriales are cultivated by human labor; naturales grow without cultivation
distractors: Industriales are commercial crops; naturales are residential garden plants
Industriales are harvested annually; naturales are harvested every five years
Industriales belong to the landowner; naturales belong to the tenant farmer
questions.explanation: Fructus industriales ('fruits of industry') are crops produced by human labor and cultivation — corn, wheat, vegetables. They are personal property. Fructus naturales ('fruits of nature') grow naturally without cultivation — trees, grass, wildflowers. They are real property because they grow from the land itself. This distinction determines whether crops transfer with the land or remain the grower's property.

questions.question_text: A life tenant plants an apple orchard on the property. The life tenant dies two years later, before the trees bear fruit. Who owns the orchard trees?
questions.correct_answer: The remainderman, because the trees are fructus naturales (real property)
distractors: The life tenant's estate, because the tenant planted them
The remainderman, but only after reimbursing planting costs
The life tenant's heirs, because planted trees are personal property
questions.explanation: Apple trees are perennial plants — once established, they grow naturally year after year without annual replanting. They are fructus naturales and therefore real property. When the life estate ends, all real property passes to the remainderman. Unlike annual crops (emblements), perennial trees and orchards are not classified as the planter's personal property. The life tenant's estate has no claim to the trees, though a life tenant could have harvested fruit during their lifetime.

questions.question_text: The process of converting personal property into real property by permanently attaching it to the land is called:
questions.correct_answer: Annexation
distractors: Severance
Accession
Dedication
questions.explanation: Annexation is the act of attaching personal property to real property so that it becomes part of the real estate. For example, purchasing lumber (personal property) and using it to build a deck permanently attached to a house converts it to real property through annexation. Severance is the reverse — removing something from real property to make it personal property. Accession relates to property acquired through additions. Dedication is the transfer of private land for public use.

questions.question_text: A property owner signs a contract to sell mineral rights beneath their land to a mining company while retaining the surface rights. This transaction involves:
questions.correct_answer: Severance
distractors: Annexation
Condemnation
Dedication
questions.explanation: When mineral rights are sold separately from the surface estate, this is severance — the separation of a component of real property into a distinct ownership interest. The minerals remain in the ground, but the right to extract them is now severed from the surface ownership. This creates two separate estates: the surface estate and the mineral estate. Severance of mineral rights is common in states with significant oil, gas, or mining activity.

questions.question_text: A contractor purchases copper pipe from a supply store and installs it as permanent plumbing in a new home. Later, a creditor with a judgment against the contractor personally attempts to seize the pipe. The creditor's claim will MOST likely fail because:
questions.correct_answer: The pipe became real property through annexation when installed
distractors: Building materials are exempt from creditor claims under federal law
The pipe is a trade fixture belonging to the contractor's business
Plumbing cannot be seized while a structure is occupied
questions.explanation: When the contractor permanently installed the copper pipe in the home, it was annexed — converted from personal property (pipe at a store) to real property (part of the plumbing system). A personal judgment creditor of the contractor cannot seize real property that belongs to the homeowner. The pipe is no longer the contractor's personal property. This illustrates why the annexation/severance distinction has practical legal consequences beyond just buyer-seller disputes.

questions.question_text: Which document is used to transfer ownership of real property from one party to another?
questions.correct_answer: A deed
distractors: A bill of sale
A promissory note
A mortgage
questions.explanation: A deed is the legal instrument that transfers ownership (title) of real property. A bill of sale transfers personal property. A promissory note is a borrower's promise to repay a loan. A mortgage (or deed of trust) pledges real property as collateral for a loan but does not transfer ownership. Understanding which document transfers which type of property is fundamental to real estate practice.

questions.question_text: A homeowner sells their house, which includes a built-in hot tub and a portable above-ground hot tub sitting on the patio. How should these two items be handled in the transaction?
questions.correct_answer: Built-in transfers by deed; portable one transfers by bill of sale
distractors: Both transfer by deed because both are located on the property
Both need a bill of sale; hot tubs are personal property
Built-in transfers by deed; portable one cannot be included
questions.explanation: The built-in hot tub, permanently installed with plumbing and electrical connections, is a fixture — part of the real property transferred by deed. The portable above-ground hot tub, sitting on the patio without permanent attachment, is personal property. If included in the sale, it must be transferred by a separate bill of sale. This is a common example of a mixed transaction where both types of property change hands simultaneously.

questions.question_text: A seller's home sale includes the property, a washer and dryer, and a storage building on a trailer frame. The purchase contract lists all items. At closing, the attorney should prepare:
questions.correct_answer: A deed for the home; bill of sale for personal property
distractors: A single deed listing all items in the transaction
A deed for home and shed; bill of sale for appliances
Three separate deeds, one per category of property
questions.explanation: The home is real property transferred by deed. The washer, dryer, and storage building on a trailer frame are personal property — the appliances are freestanding and the storage building is not permanently attached to the land (it sits on a mobile trailer frame). All personal property items transfer by bill of sale. The storage building's trailer frame is the key detail — a permanent foundation would make it real property, but a trailer frame means it is movable and therefore personal property.

questions.question_text: Why is it important to use a bill of sale rather than a deed to transfer personal property in a real estate transaction?
questions.correct_answer: Deeds are recorded publicly and designed only for real property transfers
distractors: Bills of sale provide title insurance coverage that deeds cannot
Personal property on a deed would be subject to property taxes
Federal law prohibits listing personal property on any recorded deed
questions.explanation: Deeds are specifically designed to transfer real property and are recorded in the county's public records to establish chain of title. Including personal property on a deed can create title search confusion and is legally improper. A bill of sale is the appropriate instrument for personal property — it documents the transfer without cluttering public records. Additionally, title insurance policies cover real property only, so personal property on a deed would not receive coverage.
